speaker: interviewer
text: --Capetown. Santa, this is a-- an exciting part where it's midnight, the train jerks, you wake up. Tell us what happened then.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so the train stopped, and I hear voices outside. Looking-- not-- I didn't see anything, just voices outside. And all of a sudden, I hear a clattering next compartment. Well, seeing that I was up, I went to the toilet, and I saw two-- two people in there. And when I came back, I went back to bed. But I heard a big, big knock-- knock on my door. I could-- I-- I-- I couldn't understand what it was. So in my curlers-- because I put curlers in my hair, I wanted to look nice for this-- for the next day. In my curlers and my dressing gown pulled over me, I opened the door, and there was Jack Pelham standing. Not that short, but short.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I didn't know what to say. He had a bouquet of flowers. But the bouquet of flowers had come from the dining room. He had took-- took-- took them all, and he didn't want to come empty handed. Anyhow, I didn't know what to do, what to say. And he-- and he says, can't I come in? I said, fine. I made a place for him. We sat in, and we didn't know what to say. So I told him, Joe-- I told him the regards from his family. And I told him a little bit what happened in [INAUDIBLE], while we-- where we-- where we were-- where I was, and how they entertained me, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all we-- all he said is-- well, he said, in ten days, we're getting married. I said, yes. And I looked at this man I'm getting married to. And I didn't know what was going to be. So I said, I want you to make me a promise.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, yes, what is it? And I said, I want you to promise me not to make me a baby for two years. Because we didn't have pills. We didn't have-- what do you call the-- the rubber thing? The contraception.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't have-- and at that time, a woman dependent on a man. And I didn't-- now I said-- but why, he said? I said, look, we are two strangers getting married. Please God, it will work out all right. But in case it doesn't, why should a child suffer? In two years time, we will have had enough time to get used to each other, and to see whether it is worthwhile bringing a child into the world, into this-- into this world-- whether it is right that we should bring a child. If after two years we are all right, then it is OK. Because we'll give the-- the-- the full care to the child. And he very earnestly said, I promise. And that was the conversation. And then he says, I would like to introduce you to my friend, Solly. I nearly got a shock. He brought his-- a chaperone with him. And sure enough, I said, but I don't want to meet Mister-- I had my-- my dressing gown over me like that. I-- I said, but I would like to get dressed first.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took my curlers out, got ready, and Mister-- then he came to fetch me, and Mr. King met me. Apparently I made a wonderful, wonderful impression on Mr. King. Mr King was a married man with three children, and he was my father figure. He was wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in their house, actually. And the Kings were wonderful to us. They were absolutely marvelous. And we're still friends with their children, because they are no more, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now to come to the Holocaust. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I wrote to my parents immediately, and I told them that I found happiness, because my fa-- my husband was just wonderful. We sat for one year telling each other's history. His history-- and he has a history-- he hasn't got a Holocaust history. But he has a history like a Jew, wandering from place to place and eventually finding paradise in Southern Rhodesia. And there he settled.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he told me about his experience and I told him about mine, as I was telling you until now. And so it was. And we had children. Our eldest child died. She had a hairlip. We went to Johannesburg to have it operated on, and it was operated, and three days later, she was-- she died. She must have contracted some disease at-- in the hospital. And-- that-- apparently it went inside and burnt her up. The poor child was screaming her head off. And that is what I needed.

speaker: interviewee
text: However, we were-- we were devastated, of course. That was our Barbara. And then, nine months later, our eldest child-- our other child, which of course now I call our eldest one, Ruth, who is the mother of three now. And then Naomi was born. That was by the time 1944. And the war ended 1945. I had a happy life, actually, never forgetting my family. Ne-- always thinking of them. All of a sudden, we heard the-- the-- the-- the horror stories, what is happening. And I was just hoping that my parents would be all right. I didn't know what was the matter. Actually, in 1940, a year after we were married-- I didn't have children until-- until two years later, by the way. Although I already wanted one a year later, but it just didn't happen. But in 1940, when Hitler marched-- when the Nazis marched into Paris and took it without a shot, because it was declared an open city, I-- I-- I became hysteric. I became depressed. And I had to go away to-- to rally. And I went with Mrs. King to-- to-- Beira so that I should have a bit of a-- a holiday. I became very frightened, you know. And although my husband tried everything to make me happy, took a few months to-- to put me straight. And then I waited. I waited. But when, in 1942, we heard on the wireless how Hitler was ranting-- [SPEAKING GERMAN]. They're still laughing, but they won't laugh for long, the Juden. They won't laugh long. And of course, you can imagine what effect it had on me. But yet you hoped, maybe they are-- they were somewhere else. And we hoped and hoped. Then, in 1941 or '40-- '42, I received through the Red Cross-- oh yes, I was-- from the-- from the-- from the time that the war started, I could write through the Red Cross. 10 words. And I found if I would write in German, it-- I could get in more. Somehow you could-- can express yourself 10-- in 10 words more in German than in French, because ne pas la and all this. So I did it in German. And we were corresponding until 1941, I think it was. My parents were sending me this-- this-- I was sending to them, and they were sending to me, through the Red Cross. In the Red Cross, they-- they-- they did tell me that Leon was drafted into the French Army. And because he knew French and-- and German. So he was drafted as a Dolmetscher, an interpreter. But what I didn't know-- eventually I got to know this-- but what I didn't know in the beginning was that the stalag-- not the stalag, that the regiment that my brother was in, the French regiment-- regiment that my brother was in-- was taken [NON-ENGLISH], as they say, because there-- there was not a shot fired.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- they were taken into Germany, into a stalag. And my brother, being the Jew, was not looked upon as a Jew, but a French war prisoner. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he survived because he was a French war prisoner, you understand?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Frenchman there, you see. So he survived. Although it wasn't pleasant, he said. It was horrible. But he survived. So he was in a stalag, and the letters came from him from the stalag. And he told me-- yes, all of the sudden, the letters from my parents came back, address unknown. And all-- all the others that I had sent already came back, address unknown. All I could do was hoping that they were alive. And they were alive at that time. What had happened was that they knew Ignatz, Renia's brother-in-law. They were friends. Ignatz and the rabbi, his other brother, were going into the-- into the country to hide. And they urged my parents to go also. And so they went, and they were hiding until 1940-- early 1942. They were in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the meantime, Leon was writing to me through the Red Cross. And in one letter he said, I have a letter from Chaim from-- what do you call it? Concentration camp in Oberschlesien. What had happened, I found out later, in 1940 or '41-- it must have been in there-- Chaim, the youngest one who was-- who was in the restaurant, he and a cousin of his who was born in France-- Fren-- he-- and the father was a Frenchman, but he was French naturalized. Also from Poland, originally.

speaker: interviewee
text: This young boy, Ruben, and Chaim got together, and they decided that seeing that in Fran-- in Spain, we have got a house there, that they would walk, because there was no-- no--

speaker: interviewer
text: No transport.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the war, you see? They would walk right from France, from Paris, right through to the border in Spain, and go and live in France. It was neutral.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Spain.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Spain, sorry, in Spain. That was neutral. And these two youngsters, 16, 17, oh, I don't know. They were-- it was in, let's say '41, then Chaim was 18, 19. And the other one was more or less the same age.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they walked and walked and walked and walked and they came to Vichy. And in Vichy, the French police told, who are you? What are you doing? Papers. And papers, my brother-- what papers did he have? He was a refugee.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other one, although he was a French born youngster, both of them were given over to the gestapo. And the gestapo arrested them and took them to Oberschlesien, to a camp. My brother, Leon-- then they could-- he wrote to my brother, Leon. The Red Cross arranged it. They knew where my brother Leon was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were in correspondence for six months. So Leon knew that Chaim was in Oberschlesien in a camp. But after six months, no more news, until today. He was-- he was a rebel. My brother Chaim was a rebel. And he must have stood up. And they must have killed him, you know, knocked him or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after the six months corresponding with Leon, there was nothing anymore. And Cha-- and Leon wrote me that, no more news from Chaim. No news from parents. So I was in correspondence with my brother. He knew my address all the time, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now-- when-- life went on. Life went on. And in 1940-- '45, the war ended. Everybody was jubilating. And I said, what are they jubilating? Look at what-- look at what happened. How can people jubilate like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then came the news of Auschwitz. When we heard of Auschwitz, I still didn't know that my parents were killed in Auschwitz. What had happened-- now I go back. My parents are now in the [FRENCH], in the field, in the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Country.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the country.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a country. They are working for the-- for the-- on the fields, which is natural, they should. You see, the French people don't like the Jews very much. The same, the Catholicism. They are a really strong Catholic country. But they hate the Boche more. They call the Germans the Boche, because the Boche has always been fighting the Ger-- the-- the-- the frontiers have always been shifted. The French had. The Germans came and took it. The French went back. And that went on for centuries, from Charlemagne onwards, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was always the Strasbourg belong to France, and then it belong to Germany, et cetera, et cetera. Now it belongs to France. You see? But it was old Strasbourg is German. So there was always this fight, until now. Now, thank god, they are brothers. Not in arms. Now it-- I hope it will last, the united Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the thing is that when they were there in 1940-- early 1942, they were really happy to be there because they were looked after with Ignatz and Shlomo, the other brother. And the Puterflams, they were also there. They were all together. Wonderful! My mother developed toothache, that I heard from the father of Ruben, who is a cousin to my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was also an Erder, an Erder. So my mother developed toothpaste. And instead of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Toothache?

speaker: interviewee
text: Toothache, yeah. Instead of taking a pair of tweezers or whatever, pull out that offending tooth, she must go and see a dentist. She had a few teeth left. She must go and see a dentist in Paris. So they went to Paris. And they contacted my-- Ruben's father. That's the-- you know, the Ruben who got killed individually in Oberschlesien. Ruben's father. Now, they-- they were pleased to see each other. And they said that there were-- that they are there, working. My mother must have a dentist, and he will see to it. And they went home.

speaker: interviewer
text: The next day-- Sh-- his name was Shia. Sh-- the father of Ruben. Shia the cousin went there to talk about the dentist. There was nobody. The door was wide open. That is what I heard from this-- from this cousin, you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eventually, we went-- we visited him after the war. He was still alive then. The door was wide open. There were crumbs as if they were being-- had eaten just breakfast and was left on the table. He was astounded.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went to the concierge. There is always a concierge at the door. He said, tell me, what happened to the Erders? And he said, the gestapo came to fetch them. He gave them away. And they had took them. And you know what they did at that time? They put them in a huge cinema. We had that. I saw that on-- on film sometime. Because after the war, I had to see everything that pertained to the Holocaust. I had to ex-- expurge it. I had to get it out. They must-- probably must have taken them to an assembly place, which usually was a huge cinema. Where there were women and children and babies and old people and men. And everything was there assembled. And after-- and there were only a few toilets, you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a certain time, these people were transported to a camp. It was probably Drancy. That was a camp nearby. It was probably there. This is all probabilities, but I'm putting two and two together, because I had to know. I had to do this. And I know one thing, that in July-- you see, the Germans kept--

speaker: interviewer
text: Records.

speaker: interviewee
text: Records. I know one thing, that in July, 1942, my mother was gassed. My brother found that out when he eventually came out of the stalag. So-- and my father in September. No date. But my mother in July. Also a date, but I forgot. 12, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what happened then was-- my-- the-- the-- so they were taken away. And you know the journey on the-- on the-- in the cattle trucks? Do I have to tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this slender woman, this sickly woman, she didn't suffocate. She was gassed. She didn't suffocate and like many others, have an early death. Maybe it would've been better than to have to go through all these indignities-- taking off your clothes and being assaulted, your left, your right. And then after the war, my brother was freed. Americans or the-- the English, whoever came. I think it was the Americans. And there was-- was no transport. So these soldiers, emaciated, had to walk wherever they lived. And they walked and walked days to Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their feet were bleeding, my brother tells me. He was very young still, at that time. And he arrived, and he goes to Aubervilliers. He wants to go to his place to see his parents, you see? He didn't know what had happened. And he comes, there are strangers living there. And he says, but where are my parents? Nobody knew, of course, you see? And then he stop-- then there was a place where all these-- what do you call these people who came out of cam-- out of Holocaust camps, and then they were ac-- accommodated? Displaced. The displaced.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to one of those displaced places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Camps. Camps or places, whatever. And there he started asking, where can one find out about survivors, you see? And eventually, it took some time, there were lists of people from Auschwitz. And there he found out that my mother was gassed in July, and my father in September. And he had to write that to me. And then I became very ill. I actually had a nervous breakdown. And at that time, I took two children, Ruth and Naomi. Ruthie was born in '42, so she was three years old. And Naomi was a small child, a baby. Now, I became quite ill.

speaker: interviewer
text: And people said, but what is the matter with you? They were not alone. There were 6 million! 6 million! I said, I don't know 6 million. I only know my family. Where is my family? And I was very ill.

speaker: interviewee
text: Until Ruthie got ill. And she got ill, my husband called a doctor. She must go to hospital. And I woke up. I said, if my mother were alive, she wouldn't want me to mourn now. I have to look after the future generation, to carry on our race, to carry on my mother and father's genes. It goes through me to this child, and then further, to the-- to the next generation. And this is what my mother would want. Always talked about my mother. I don't known why I didn't talk about my father, because he would have-- would have wanted that also. And so it was that the child was taken to hospital. I recovered very quickly. I am a very strong person. And-- and in no time-- I didn't get over it. When-- immediately. When the-- when the films came, Rise Whatever film, whichever had a little bit of Nazism or of the German Reich in it, I had to see. My husband saw one, and he says, I've had enough. I can't. I went during the day. I went whenever it was showing. I had to get it out of me. And I sat there stony faced, and slowly, slowly, I realized life must go on. And this is how I sort of, you know, carried on with my life. And-- and I came to myself. I just carried on. And I raised the most wonderful, wonderful, family. Three most wonderful children who are compensating for everything. And they also-- we are very lucky. They have wonderful husbands, all three of them. It is luck.

words.text: I am a very strong person.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you believe in spiritualism, you say my mother and father are sitting up there and doing it. I don't believe that, of course. But people do believe this, you see? When my first child was born, I didn't know whether my mother was alive or not, so we called her Barbara because I liked the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when Ruthie was born, it was '42. I didn't know whether they were alive or not, so I called her a name that I liked, and that was Ruth. My husband agreed with everything. And when the second one was born, Naomi goes very well with Ruth. Ah!

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Aviva was just following up, I had a feeling I did not want to call after all this unhappiness my mother had experienced. She was a wonderful person, full of feelings and full of-- of-- she had strength in her, how she pushed me away, knowing, feeling, that she will never see me. That is strong. She wanted me to be saved, and I was saved. And eventually, of course, my brother came. You know, he came over to be-- it was great rejoicing and great crying and so on. And now we have got this wonderful family. We have got eight grandchildren, four boys and four girls. And they are just wonderful. We are one marvelous, big family. We come together on Friday night. I feel that Friday nights are the basis for the Jewish people. That Friday night, which is only one week away. You don't have to wait one year for Christmas and all, or one year for New Year, or whatever it is. You have a week between. A week is not easily forgotten, and it stays in you. Every Friday night, those candles are standing on the beautiful white tablecloth with the two kitke stand-- lying there, all traditional. I believe in tradition. Carry on, carry on, carry on. And the grandchildren saying the-- the kiddush now. They are saying it now. And the-- the youngest grandchild breaking the kitke and making HaMotzi over it. And making the Birkat Hamazon after a wonderful meal, being happy together. That holds the family. This is Judaism. This is what carries it on. We-- OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Santa, thank you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Is that all?

speaker: interviewer
text: --very much for telling your story so vividly. Shortly we'll be showing some pictures and bringing in your fa--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell us, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: What would you like to know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who this person is.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother. It must have been in 1913 or '14, something like this. As you can see, the dress is from that period.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [LAUGHTER] Tell us about this person, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is my father. His name was Simcha Meilech Erder. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is of my parents and brothers, taken after I had left Paris. It was taken in-- end of 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken prior to my leaving Paris, when Ignatz Perlman, my brother-in-law, came to see me-- if I'm good enough for Jack Pelham. In the middle stands Simon Puterflam, the one who went for the midwife for me in 1918. And that's his wife and daughter. [PAUSES] This photo was taken recently at the celebration of the marriage of one of my grandsons. It shows my three daughters and their three husbands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewee
text: This one is also taken at the same occasion, at the wedding of this bridegroom. All our eight grandchildren are here. The bridegroom is second from left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. Fun.

speaker: interviewee
text: So--

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. You're on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, here we are assembled. Nearly all of my family is here, except a daughter, her husband, who live in Johannesburg. And the three-- our three grandchildren from Ruth, our eldest daughter, who is sitting here to the left in the-- in the pink. She's in the pink. [LAUGHTER] And so this is really wonderful to know that after the Holocaust, we are here, a family of 17 at the moment, all out of my-- my father and mother, who were killed at-- at Auschwitz. And now we have got here our granddaughter, the eldest daughter of Naomi, who is not here at the moment. And she is married to Clifford, sitting next to her. And they are going to make us the gr-- the proud great grandparents, and the fourth generation from Maita and Simon Erder. And she's going to tell us a few things now.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just think that this is the most fantastic opportunity. We all enjoy Granny telling us the most wonderful stories, some of them sad, but some of them very happy. And I just think it's fantastic that my baby and the following generations will be able to actually listen to the story from my grandma, which will be great. Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [PAUSES]

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER] Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, good.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Take 2? [LAUGHTER]
